summary: Gruber responds to Rob Griffiths' decision to never update his iPhone past 1.0.2 in order to keep third-party apps. Gruber argues that Apple locked down the iPhone file system primarily to prevent SIM-unlocking that would violate carrier exclusivity contracts, and that blocking third-party apps was a side effect rather than the goal. He pushes back on the claim that Apple sees no upside to third-party development, suggesting they may simply not be ready yet. He concludes that Apple has no obligation to the grassroots developer community it never invited, and that the only real pressure consumers can exert is by not buying iPhones.
key_points: The iPhone 1.1.1 update locked down file system access primarily to prevent SIM-unlocking, with third-party app removal being a consequence rather than the intent.
Apple's silence on future developer access doesn't mean they'll never open the platform — it follows their standard policy of not pre-announcing plans.
The grassroots iPhone developer community was never invited or encouraged by Apple, so claiming Apple 'screwed them over' is absurd.
The only effective way to pressure Apple into opening iPhone development is to stop buying iPhones — money talks.
There are legitimate trade-offs to opening the platform, including the link between third-party app access and SIM-unlocking vulnerabilities.
money_quotes: The third-party hacks were fun to play with, but there isn't a single one that I miss. Not one.
If you think Apple could have simply let that be without breaking the contracts they've signed guaranteeing exclusivity to their carrier partners around the world, you're nuts.
You can argue that Apple should allow it anyway, but you can't argue that that's not a trade-off.
The idea that Apple has screwed over or even somehow been rude to the grassroots iPhone developer community is absurd.
Apple never asked for nor encouraged iPhone software development, and the fact that it happened anyway put Apple under zero obligation to support it.
If 'third-party software development' is one of your requirements for a phone, buying something other than an iPhone is exactly what Apple wants you to do.
There's only one way to pressure Apple into opening up iPhone development, and it isn't by developing underground iPhone apps. It's by not buying iPhones.
Money talks, and right now, there doesn't seem to be a shortage of people willing to give Apple money in exchange for a completely closed iPhone.
key_insight: Apple's iPhone lockdown was a rational business decision to honor carrier contracts, not an attack on developers, and the only leverage consumers have is their wallets.
